title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who share their relationship with food and cooking. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Food for Thought: Four People on Cooking and Eating

texts.A:
### Rosa, 36, Interior Designer
[43]I learned to cook from my grandmother, who could transform the most basic ingredients into something spectacular.[/43] She never used recipes – everything was done by instinct and memory, and watching her was like watching magic. I've tried to carry that same spirit into my own kitchen. [47]Cooking is how I show love – when someone is going through a difficult time, my first instinct is to feed them.[/47] I find the whole process therapeutic: the chopping, the stirring, the smells. [51]The one thing I refuse to do is cook the same dish twice in a month – variety is essential and there are too many wonderful recipes in the world to repeat yourself.[/51]
texts.B:
### Dan, 25, IT Support Technician
[44]I was a fussy eater as a child – I refused to eat anything green and survived on pasta and cheese for years.[/44] My flatmates persuaded me to start cooking properly and it genuinely changed my relationship with food. Now I'll try almost anything. [48]I follow several food bloggers online and have discovered cuisines I never would have encountered otherwise, like Georgian and Ethiopian food.[/48] [52]The thing I enjoy most about cooking is that it's one of the few activities where I'm completely off my phone.[/52] I put on some music, close all my apps, and just focus on what's in front of me. It's become my form of meditation.
texts.C:
### Ananya, 52, University Lecturer
Food is inseparable from culture, and I find that fascinating. [45]I grew up between two countries – India and the UK – and food was the main way my family kept our cultural identity alive.[/45] My mother would spend entire Saturdays making dishes from memory that she hadn't eaten since she left home. [49]I now research the history of food as part of my academic work and I believe every dish tells a story about migration, trade, and power.[/49] [53]The aspect of modern food culture that concerns me most is the homogenisation of diets and the loss of traditional recipes as older generations pass away.[/53]
texts.D:
### Luke, 40, Personal Trainer
[46]I approach food primarily as fuel – I'm disciplined about what I eat because I understand directly how diet affects performance and recovery.[/46] I'm not obsessive about it, but I do track my protein and avoid highly processed food as much as possible. [50]The hardest part of my healthy eating routine is maintaining it when I'm travelling or eating out, where the options are often limited.[/50] I think there's too much confusing and contradictory nutritional advice available online and it ends up paralysing people instead of helping them. My advice is simple: eat real food, mostly plants, and don't overthink it.

questions.question: was taught to cook by a relative who worked entirely from memory rather than written instructions?
questions.explanation: Rosa's grandmother never used recipes and cooked by instinct and memory.

questions.question: used to have a very limited diet but has since become more adventurous with food?
questions.explanation: Dan was a fussy eater as a child but his relationship with food changed when he started cooking properly.

questions.question: sees food as a way of maintaining a connection to their cultural heritage?
questions.explanation: Ananya says food was the main way her family kept their cultural identity alive between two countries.

questions.question: views food mainly in terms of its practical benefits for their physical performance?
questions.explanation: Luke approaches food primarily as fuel because he understands how diet affects performance and recovery.

questions.question: uses cooking as a way to support people they care about emotionally?
questions.explanation: Rosa says cooking is how she shows love and her first instinct when someone is struggling is to feed them.

questions.question: has discovered new types of food through following people on the internet?
questions.explanation: Dan discovered cuisines like Georgian and Ethiopian food by following food bloggers online.

questions.question: studies the cultural and historical significance of food professionally?
questions.explanation: Ananya researches the history of food as part of her academic work and sees every dish as a story.

questions.question: finds it difficult to maintain their usual eating habits when away from their normal routine?
questions.explanation: Luke says the hardest part is maintaining healthy eating while travelling or eating out where options are limited.

questions.question: makes a deliberate effort to keep variety in their cooking?
questions.explanation: Rosa refuses to cook the same dish twice in a month because she values variety and there are too many recipes to repeat.

questions.question: finds that cooking helps them take a break from constant digital activity?
questions.explanation: Dan says cooking is one of the few activities where he is completely off his phone, treating it as a form of meditation.
